Section: Survey papers
Article Name: A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge
Author List: Jiawei Gu; Xuhui Jiang; Zhichao Shi; Hexiang Tan; Xuehao Zhai; Chengjin Xu; Wei Li; Yinghan Shen; Shengjie Ma; Honghao Liu; Saizhuo Wang; Kun Zhang; Yuanzhuo Wang; Wen Gao; Lionel Ni; Jian Guo
About: This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm, defining the field, organizing prior work into a unified framework, and focusing especially on how to build more reliable judge systems. It reviews methods for improving consistency and reducing bias, proposes ways to evaluate judge reliability, and introduces a benchmark for systematic meta-evaluation.

Section: Survey papers
Article Name: From Generation to Judgment: Opportunities and Challenges of LLM-as-a-judge
Author List: Dawei Li; Bohan Jiang; Liangjie Huang; Alimohammad Beigi; Chengshuai Zhao; Zhen Tan; Amrita Bhattacharjee; Yuxuan Jiang; Canyu Chen; Tianhao Wu; Kai Shu; Lu Cheng; Huan Liu
About: This paper is a comprehensive survey of the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm, defining it from both input and output perspectives and organizing the field around three core questions: what to judge, how to judge, and how to benchmark judge systems. It also synthesizes the main opportunities, open challenges, and future directions for using LLMs as evaluators in open-ended AI and NLP settings.

Section: Survey papers
Article Name: Leveraging Large Language Models for NLG Evaluation: Advances and Challenges
Author List: Zhen Li; Xiaohan Xu; Shen Tao; Can Xu; Jia-Chen Gu; Yuxuan Lai; Chongyang Tao; Shuai Ma
About: This paper surveys how large language models are being used for natural language generation evaluation, proposing a taxonomy of LLM-based evaluation methods and comparing their strengths and limitations across NLG settings. It also highlights key open challenges—especially bias, robustness, domain specificity, and the lack of unified evaluation standards—to guide future work on fairer and more reliable evaluation.

Section: Survey papers
Article Name: LLMs-as-Judges: A Comprehensive Survey on LLM-based Evaluation Methods
Author List: Haitao Li, Qian Dong, Junjie Chen, Huixue Su, Yujia Zhou, Qingyao Ai, Ziyi Ye, Yiqun Liu
About: This paper surveys the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm from five angles—functionality, methodology, applications, meta-evaluation, and limitations—offering a broad framework for understanding why, where, and how LLM judges are used. It also emphasizes how to evaluate judge systems themselves and outlines major limitations and future research directions for making LLM-based evaluation more trustworthy and useful in practice.

Section: Survey papers
Article Name:
LLM-based NLG Evaluation:
Current Status and Challenges
Author List: Mingqi Gao; Xinyu Hu; Xunjian Yin; Jie Ruan; Xiao Pu; Xiaojun Wan
About: This paper surveys the emerging area of LLM-based evaluation for natural language generation, organizing prior work into four main approaches: LLM-derived metrics, prompting-based evaluation, fine-tuned judge models, and human–LLM collaborative evaluation. It also analyzes the strengths and weaknesses of these approaches and highlights key open challenges for making LLM-based evaluation more robust, efficient, and reliable.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
Author List: Lianmin Zheng, Wei-Lin Chiang, Ying Sheng, Siyuan Zhuang, Zhanghao Wu, Yonghao Zhuang, Zi Lin, Zhuohan Li, Dacheng Li, Eric P. Xing, Hao Zhang, Joseph E. Gonzalez, Ion Stoica
About: This paper is a foundational study of the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm, showing that strong models such as GPT-4 can approximate human preferences on open-ended chatbot evaluation while also documenting key judge biases, including position, verbosity, and self-enhancement bias. It introduces MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena as benchmark frameworks for evaluating conversational assistants and argues that LLM judges can provide a scalable, explainable complement to human evaluation for open-ended systems.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: PandaLM: An Automatic Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Instruction Tuning Optimization
Author List: Yidong Wang; Zhuohao Yu; Zhengran Zeng; Linyi Yang; Cunxiang Wang; Hao Chen; Chaoya Jiang; Rui Xie; Jindong Wang; Xing Xie; Wei Ye; Shikun Zhang; Yue Zhang
About: This paper introduces PandaLM, a fine-tuned judge LLM designed to automatically evaluate instruction-tuned models by comparing candidate responses along both objective and subjective dimensions such as clarity, conciseness, and instruction adherence. It argues that PandaLM can provide a cheaper, reproducible alternative to API-based or human evaluation, and shows that using it to guide hyperparameter selection improves downstream model quality.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: From Crowdsourced Data to High-Quality Benchmarks: Arena-Hard and BenchBuilder Pipeline
Author List: Tianle Li; Wei-Lin Chiang; Evan Frick; Lisa Dunlap; Tianhao Wu; Banghua Zhu; Joseph E. Gonzalez; Ion Stoica
About: This paper introduces BenchBuilder, an automated pipeline for constructing difficult open-ended evaluation benchmarks, and uses it to create Arena-Hard-Auto, a benchmark that aligns closely with human preference rankings while better separating model performance than MT-Bench. It is relevant to judge LLMs because such benchmarks provide the evaluation infrastructure needed to test whether LLM judges rank model outputs in ways that track human preferences, especially on challenging prompts.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: MLLM-as-a-Judge: Assessing Multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge with Vision-Language Benchmark
Author List: Dongping Chen; Ruoxi Chen; Shilin Zhang; Yaochen Wang; Yinuo Liu; Huichi Zhou; Qihui Zhang; Yao Wan; Pan Zhou; Lichao Sun
About: This paper introduces MLLM-as-a-Judge, a benchmark for evaluating whether multimodal LLMs can act as judges across vision-language tasks using scoring, pairwise comparison, and batch ranking settings. It shows that while models can be reasonably human-aligned in pairwise comparison, they still exhibit substantial reliability problems—including bias, hallucination, and inconsistency—in other judging modes, making the paper highly relevant to judge-LLM research in the multimodal setting.

Section: Practitioner guides and blog posts
Article Name: Using LLM-as-a-Judge For Evaluation: A Complete Guide
Author List: Hamel Husain
About: A practitioner-focused guide to building LLM judges for real evaluation workflows, with concrete advice on rubric design, calibration, and common implementation mistakes. It is especially useful for teams deploying evals in production settings.

Section: Practitioner guides and blog posts
Article Name: LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations in the Age of GenAI
Author List: Hanchung Lee
About: A practical overview of how LLM-as-a-judge fits into the broader evolution of text evaluation, with actionable guidance and prompt ideas for improving evaluator reliability. It is a good intuition-building resource for applied GenAI evaluation.

Section: Practitioner guides and blog posts
Article Name: LLM-as-a-judge: a complete guide to using LLMs for evaluations
Author List: Evidently AI
About: A concise applied guide explaining why LLM-as-a-judge is popular, how to build an evaluator, and what tradeoffs and alternatives practitioners should consider. It is written for teams evaluating LLM-powered products rather than for a research audience.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluations?
Author List: Cheng-Han Chiang; Hung-yi Lee
About: This paper is an early foundational study of LLM-as-evaluator, asking whether large language models can serve as an alternative to human evaluation by rating text using the same instructions and questions given to human judges. Across story generation and adversarial-attack evaluation, it finds that LLM ratings are broadly consistent with expert human judgments and relatively stable across prompt formatting and sampling choices.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Keeping Humans in the Loop: Human-Centered Automated Annotation with Generative AI
Author List: Nick Pangakis, Sam Wolken
About: This paper argues that generative AI can support large-scale automated annotation, but only within a human-centered validation framework that checks model performance across tasks rather than assuming LLM labels are uniformly reliable. Replicating 27 annotation tasks across 11 computational social science datasets, it finds that GPT-4 often produces strong labels but shows substantial task-to-task variation, so human oversight and careful validation remain essential.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Neither Valid nor Reliable? Investigating the Use of LLMs as Judges
Author List: Khaoula Chehbouni; Mohammed Haddou; Jackie Chi Kit Cheung; Golnoosh Farnadi
About: This position paper critically examines the use of LLMs as judges, arguing that current enthusiasm has outpaced rigorous evidence about their validity and reliability as evaluators. Drawing on social-science measurement theory, it challenges four core assumptions—human proxy validity, evaluator capability, scalability, and cost-effectiveness—and uses applications such as summarization, data annotation, and safety alignment to argue for more responsible judge-LLM evaluation practices.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
Author List: Sunishchal Dev; Andrew Sloan; Joshua Kavner; Nicholas Kong; Morgan Sandler
About: This paper introduces the Judge Reliability Harness, an open-source framework for systematically stress-testing LLM judges across free-response and agentic tasks by generating validation suites for both binary and ordinal judgments. Evaluating four state-of-the-art judges across safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic benchmarks, it finds substantial sensitivity to perturbations such as formatting, paraphrasing, verbosity, and label flips, showing that no tested judge is uniformly reliable across settings.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Who Validates the Validators? Aligning LLM-Assisted Evaluation of LLM Outputs with Human Preferences
Author List: Shreya Shankar; J. D. Zamfirescu-Pereira; Björn Hartmann; Aditya G. Parameswaran; Ian Arawjo
About: This paper introduces EvalGen, a mixed-initiative system for aligning LLM-assisted evaluation with human preferences by helping users generate and refine evaluation criteria, prompts, and code-based assertions. It is especially relevant to judge LLMs because it shows that LLM-generated evaluators must themselves be validated against human judgments, and it identifies criteria drift as a core challenge in building reliable automated evaluators.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using Gpt-4 with Better Human Alignment
Author List: Yang Liu; Dan Iter; Yichong Xu; Shuohang Wang; Ruochen Xu; Chenguang Zhu
About: This paper introduces G-Eval, a GPT-4–based framework for reference-free NLG evaluation that uses chain-of-thought prompting and a form-filling scheme to improve alignment with human judgments on summarization and dialogue tasks. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it is one of the earliest and most influential papers showing that strong LLM judges can outperform prior automatic metrics while still exhibiting important biases, including a preference for LLM-generated text.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: FLASK: Fine-grained Language Model Evaluation based on Alignment Skill Sets
Author List: Seonghyeon Ye; Doyoung Kim; Sungdong Kim; Hyeonbin Hwang; Seungone Kim; Yongrae Jo; James Thorne; Juho Kim; Minjoon Seo
About: This paper introduces FLASK, a fine-grained LLM evaluation framework that decomposes model performance into instruction-specific alignment skill sets rather than relying only on overall preference judgments. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it proposes a more interpretable and potentially more reliable way for both human and model-based evaluators to assess outputs, and shows strong correlation between model-based and human-based judgments.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Prometheus: Inducing Fine-Grained Evaluation Capability in Language Models
Author List: Seungone Kim; Jamin Shin; Yejin Cho; Joel Jang; Shayne Longpre; Hwaran Lee; Sangdoo Yun; Seongjin Shin; Sungdong Kim; James Thorne; Minjoon Seo
About: This paper introduces Prometheus, a fully open-source fine-grained evaluator LLM trained to score long-form responses against customizable rubrics and reference materials, aiming to provide a cheaper and more controllable alternative to proprietary judges like GPT-4. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it develops a dedicated judge model, shows strong agreement with human evaluators across customized rubrics, and frames rubric-conditioned evaluation as a scalable paradigm for automatic assessment.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Prometheus 2: An Open Source Language Model Specialized in Evaluating Other Language Models
Author List: Seungone Kim; Juyoung Suk; Shayne Longpre; Bill Yuchen Lin; Jamin Shin; Sean Welleck; Graham Neubig; Moontae Lee; Kyungjae Lee; Minjoon Seo
About: This paper introduces Prometheus 2, an open-source evaluator LM designed to assess other language models using both direct scoring and pairwise ranking under user-defined evaluation criteria. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it advances dedicated judge-model development and shows stronger agreement with human and proprietary LM judgments than prior open evaluator models across multiple evaluation benchmarks.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Justice or Prejudice? Quantifying Biases in LLM-as-a-Judge
Author List: Jiayi Ye, Yanbo Wang, Yue Huang, Dongping Chen, Qihui Zhang, Nuno Moniz, Tian Gao, Werner Geyer, Chao Huang, Pin-Yu Chen, Nitesh V. Chawla, Xiangliang Zhang
About: This paper systematically studies bias in LLM-as-a-Judge, identifying 12 distinct bias types and introducing CALM, an automated framework for quantifying them through controlled, principle-guided modifications to evaluation inputs. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly targets one of the field’s central validity concerns: whether judge models produce reliable evaluations or are swayed by irrelevant features and hidden systematic biases

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Are LLM-Judges Robust to Expressions of Uncertainty? Investigating the Effect of Epistemic Markers on LLM-based Evaluation
Author List: Dongryeol Lee, Yerin Hwang, Yongil Kim, Joonsuk Park, Kyomin Jung
About: This paper introduces EMBER, a benchmark for testing whether LLM judges remain reliable when evaluated outputs contain epistemic markers such as expressions of certainty or uncertainty, across both single-answer and pairwise evaluation settings. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it shows that even strong judges, including GPT-4o, are systematically biased by these markers—especially uncertainty expressions—rather than focusing only on underlying correctness.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: HD-Eval: Aligning Large Language Model Evaluators Through Hierarchical Criteria Decomposition
Author List: Yuxuan Liu, Tianchi Yang, Shaohan Huang, Zihan Zhang, Haizhen Huang, Furu Wei, Weiwei Deng, Feng Sun, Qi Zhang
About: This paper introduces HD-EVAL, a framework for aligning LLM evaluators with human preferences by decomposing evaluation into a hierarchy of fine-grained criteria, aggregating those criteria with a human-preference-guided white-box model, and pruning unimportant dimensions during iterative training. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly addresses a core problem in the area—how to make LLM-based evaluators more aligned, explainable, and trustworthy than single-prompt judging approaches.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
Author List: Peiyi Wang, Lei Li, Liang Chen, Zefan Cai, Dawei Zhu, Binghuai Lin, Yunbo Cao, Lingpeng Kong, Qi Liu, Tianyu Liu, Zhifang Sui
About: This paper shows that LLM judges suffer from a strong positional bias: simply swapping the order of candidate responses can substantially change evaluation outcomes, even enough to make weaker models appear better than stronger ones. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly identifies a core reliability threat in pairwise LLM evaluation and proposes a calibration method that improves alignment with human judgments.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Self-Preference Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge
Author List: Koki Wataoka, Tsubasa Takahashi, Ryokan Ri
About: This paper studies self-preference bias in LLM-as-a-Judge, introducing a quantitative metric for measuring the phenomenon and showing that GPT-4 systematically favors outputs that are more familiar to it. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it identifies a concrete reliability threat and argues that the bias is driven less by literal self-generation than by a preference for lower-perplexity text that better matches the judge model’s own distribution.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Beyond the Surface: Measuring Self-Preference in LLM Judgments
Author List: Zhi-Yuan Chen, Hao Wang, Xinyu Zhang, Enrui Hu, Yankai Lin
About: This paper studies self-preference bias in LLM judges and argues that existing measurements confound bias with actual response quality. It introduces the DBG score, a gold-judgment-based metric that more cleanly isolates self-preference bias, and uses it to show how factors such as model size, reasoning ability, response style, and post-training data affect judge reliability.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Section 2: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
Author List: Yann Dubois, Xuechen Li, Rohan Taori, Tianyi Zhang, Ishaan Gulrajani, Jimmy Ba, Carlos Guestrin, Percy Liang, Tatsunori B. Hashimoto
About: This paper introduces AlpacaFarm, a low-cost simulation framework for research on learning from human feedback that uses LLMs to emulate pairwise human preference judgments, provide automatic evaluation, and support reproducible experimentation with RLHF-style methods. It is relevant to judge LLMs because it shows how LLMs can act as scalable evaluators of model outputs and provides early evidence that such automated judgments can approximate human preference rankings in instruction-following settings.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Length-Controlled AlpacaEval: A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
Author List: Yann Dubois, Balázs Galambosi, Percy Liang, Tatsunori B. Hashimoto
About: This paper introduces Length-Controlled AlpacaEval, a simple regression-based method for removing length bias from LLM-based automatic evaluation, asking what preferences would look like if compared outputs had the same length. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly addresses one of the field’s best-known validity threats—evaluators rewarding verbosity rather than true quality—and shows that debiasing improves agreement with human-preference rankings.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: BiasScope: Towards Automated Detection of Bias in LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation
Author List: Peng Lai, Zhihao Ou, Yong Wang, Longyue Wang, Jian Yang, Yun Chen, Guanhua Chen
About: This paper introduces BiasScope, an automated framework for systematically discovering both known and previously unidentified biases in LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, and uses it to build JudgeBench-Pro, a more challenging robustness benchmark. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it shifts bias analysis from manually checking predefined bias types to automated large-scale bias discovery, and shows that even strong judge models still make errors on more than half of the benchmark cases.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs
Author List: Jingming Zhuo, Songyang Zhang, Xinyu Fang, Haodong Duan, Dahua Lin, Kai Chen
About: This paper introduces ProSA, a framework for measuring and analyzing how sensitive LLM performance is to prompt wording, using a new metric called PromptSensiScore and decoding confidence to study the phenomenon. It is relevant to judge LLMs because prompt sensitivity is a core reliability threat for any evaluation system built on LLM prompting, especially in subjective and reasoning-heavy judgment tasks.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: State of What Art? A Call for Multi-Prompt LLM Evaluation
Author List: Moran Mizrahi, Guy Kaplan, Dan Malkin, Rotem Dror, Dafna Shahaf, Gabriel Stanovsky
About: This paper shows that single-prompt LLM evaluation is brittle: across 6.5 million evaluations spanning 20 LLMs and 39 tasks, different instruction paraphrases can substantially change both absolute scores and relative model rankings. It is relevant to judge LLMs because it identifies prompt sensitivity as a core reliability threat and argues that judge-based evaluation should use multiple prompt variants and aggregated metrics rather than relying on a single evaluation prompt.

Section: Rubric Design, Calibration, and Evaluation Frameworks
Section 2: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: LLM-Rubric: A Multidimensional, Calibrated Approach to Automated Evaluation of Natural Language Texts
Author List: Helia Hashemi, Jason Eisner, Corby Rosset, Benjamin Van Durme, Chris Kedzie
About: This paper introduces a rubric-based evaluation framework that prompts an LLM on multiple assessment dimensions and then calibrates those outputs with a small neural model to better predict individual human judges’ ratings, including overall quality judgments. It is relevant to judge LLMs because it treats automated evaluation as a multidimensional, judge-calibrated measurement problem rather than a single raw model verdict.

Section: Rubric Design, Calibration, and Evaluation Frameworks
Section 2: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Autorubric: A Unified Framework for Rubric-Based LLM Evaluation
Author List: Delip Rao, Chris Callison-Burch
About: This paper presents Autorubric, an open-source framework that unifies rubric-based LLM evaluation, supporting binary/ordinal/nominal criteria, multi-judge aggregation, bias mitigations, few-shot calibration, and psychometric reliability metrics. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it turns many scattered judge-design choices into one integrated evaluation framework with explicit attention to reliability and production use.

Section: Rubric Design, Calibration, and Evaluation Frameworks
Section 2: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: PairEval: Open-domain Dialogue Evaluation Metric with Pairwise Comparisons
Author List: ChaeHun Park, Minseok Choi, Dohyun Lee, Jaegul Choo
About: This paper introduces PairEval, a pairwise comparison metric for open-domain dialogue evaluation built by specializing moderate-size open models to compare responses relative to one another rather than scoring them in isolation. It is relevant to judge LLMs because it advances pairwise judge-style evaluation and shows stronger correlation with human judgments and better detection of common dialogue failures than prior metrics.

Section: Rubric Design, Calibration, and Evaluation Frameworks
Section 2: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: RevisEval: Improving LLM-as-a-Judge via Response-Adapted References
Author List: Qiyuan Zhang, Yufei Wang, Tiezheng Yu, Yuxin Jiang, Chuhan Wu, Liangyou Li, Yasheng Wang, Xin Jiang, Lifeng Shang, Ruiming Tang, Fuyuan Lyu, Chen Ma
About: This paper introduces RevisEval, a judge-LLM evaluation method that first adaptively revises a candidate response to create a response-specific reference, then uses that reference to improve evaluation quality. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly targets the reliability gap between LLM judges and human evaluation, showing gains over both reference-free and traditional reference-based judging while also reducing bias.

Section: Rubric Design, Calibration, and Evaluation Frameworks
Article Name: EvalAssist: LLM-as-a-Judge Simplified
Author List: Michael Desmond, Zahra Ashktorab, Werner Geyer, Elizabeth M. Daly, Martín Santillán Cooper, Qian Pan, Rahul Nair, Nico Wagner, Tejaswini Pedapati
About: This paper presents EvalAssist, a human-centered tool for building, testing, and sharing custom LLM-as-a-judge criteria and running structured evaluation pipelines with off-the-shelf or specialized evaluator models. It is relevant to judge LLMs because it focuses on the practical workflow of creating and operationalizing custom judging criteria for real-world model evaluation and risk assessment.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Section 2: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: JudgeLM: Fine-tuned Large Language Models are Scalable Judges
Author List: Lianghui Zhu, Xinggang Wang, Xinlong Wang
About: This paper introduces JudgeLM, a family of fine-tuned LLM judges trained on a large dataset of GPT-4 judgments to provide scalable automatic evaluation for open-ended benchmarks. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it develops dedicated judge models, analyzes major judge biases such as position, knowledge, and format bias, and proposes debiasing techniques that improve agreement with stronger teacher judges.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: Auto-J: Generative Judge for Evaluating Alignment
Author List: Junlong Li, Shichao Sun, Weizhe Yuan, Run-Ze Fan, Hai Zhao, Pengfei Liu
About: This paper introduces Auto-J, a 13B generative judge model designed to evaluate alignment-oriented LLM outputs across diverse real-world scenarios, supporting both pairwise comparison and single-response evaluation while also generating natural-language critiques. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it develops a dedicated evaluator model for open-ended alignment tasks and shows strong performance across a broad 58-scenario testbed.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: An Empirical Study of LLM-as-a-Judge for LLM Evaluation: Fine-tuned Judge Model is not a General Substitute for GPT-4
Author List: Hui Huang, Yingqi Qu, Xingyuan Bu, Hongli Zhou, Jing Liu, Muyun Yang, Bing Xu, Tiejun Zhao
About: This paper presents an empirical critique of fine-tuned LLM judges, showing that although they can perform well on in-domain meta-evaluation benchmarks, they fall short of GPT-4 in generalizability, fairness, and adaptability across evaluation schemes and tasks. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it argues that many fine-tuned judge models behave more like task-specific classifiers than general evaluators, challenging the claim that they are reliable substitutes for stronger proprietary judges.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Replacing Judges with Juries: Evaluating LLM Generations with a Panel of Diverse Models
Author List: Pat Verga, Sebastian Hofstätter, Sophia Althammer, Yixuan Su, Aleksandra Piktus, Arkady Arkhangorodsky, Minjie Xu, Naomi White, Patrick Lewis
About: This paper introduces PoLL (“Panel of LLM evaluators”), showing that using a panel of diverse smaller judge models can outperform a single large judge while also reducing intra-model bias and cutting evaluation cost by more than 7×. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly proposes a new evaluation architecture for improving reliability, robustness, and cost-efficiency in LLM-based judging.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: ChatEval: Towards Better LLM-based Evaluators through Multi-Agent Debate
Author List: Chi-Min Chan, Weize Chen, Yusheng Su, Jianxuan Yu, Wei Xue, Shanghang Zhang, Jie Fu, Zhiyuan Liu
About: This paper introduces ChatEval, a multi-agent debate framework for LLM-based evaluation in which multiple LLM “referees” discuss and assess generated responses rather than relying on a single judge prompt. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it frames evaluation as a collaborative judging process and argues that multi-agent deliberation can produce more reliable, human-mimicking assessments than single-agent evaluators.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: LLMBar: Evaluating Large Language Models at Evaluating Instruction Following
Author List: Zhiyuan Zeng, Jiatong Yu, Tianyu Gao, Yu Meng, Tanya Goyal, Danqi Chen
About: This paper studies how well LLMs can act as evaluators of instruction following, introducing LLMBar, a challenging meta-evaluation benchmark containing deceptively difficult response pairs where one output follows the instruction and the other appears attractive but does not. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly evaluates the strengths and failure modes of LLM evaluators and shows that even strong judge setups still have substantial room for improvement on instruction-following assessment.

Section: Foundational papers on judge LLM systems and judge LLM benchmarks
Article Name: JudgeBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-based Judges
Author List: Sijun Tan, Siyuan Zhuang, Kyle Montgomery, William Y. Tang, Alejandro Cuadron, Chenguang Wang, Raluca Ada Popa, Ion Stoica
About: This paper introduces JudgeBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based judges on difficult response pairs in knowledge, reasoning, math, and coding, where correctness is grounded in objective answers rather than crowdsourced stylistic preference. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly targets a core validity problem in the field—whether judges can reliably identify factual and logical correctness on challenging tasks—and shows that many strong judges perform only slightly above random on this harder benchmark.

Section: Validity and reliability of judge LLMs
Article Name: Self-Taught Evaluators
Author List: Tianlu Wang, Ilia Kulikov, Olga Golovneva, Ping Yu, Weizhe Yuan, Jane Dwivedi-Yu, Richard Yuanzhe Pang, Maryam Fazel-Zarandi, Jason Weston, Xian Li
About: This paper introduces Self-Taught Evaluators, a method for improving LLM judges using only synthetic preference data rather than human annotations, by iteratively generating contrasting outputs and training the judge on its own reasoning traces and judgments. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it directly studies how evaluator models can be trained and improved as judges, and reports that the resulting model surpasses GPT-4 and matches top reward models on RewardBench without labeled preference data.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: Preference Leakage: A Contamination Problem in LLM-as-a-judge
Author List: Dawei Li, Renliang Sun, Yue Huang, Ming Zhong, Bohan Jiang, Jiawei Han, Xiangliang Zhang, Wei Wang, Huan Liu
About: This paper identifies preference leakage, a contamination problem in LLM-as-a-Judge where evaluators become biased toward models that are related to the LLM used to generate synthetic preference data, such as the same model, an inherited model, or one from the same family. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it surfaces a subtle but important validity threat in modern judge pipelines, showing that evaluation can be distorted by hidden generator–judge relatedness rather than true output quality.

Section: Systematic Biases in Judge LLMs
Article Name: LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations
Author List: Arjun Panickssery, Samuel R. Bowman, Shi Feng
About: This paper studies self-preference bias in LLM evaluators, asking whether judges favor their own generations because they can actually recognize them, and finds that models like GPT-4 and Llama 2 can identify their own outputs at non-trivial rates. It is highly relevant to judge LLMs because it links self-recognition to biased evaluation, showing that a judge’s tendency to reward familiar text can undermine unbiased automatic assessment.

Section: Curated resource repositories and GitHub lists
Article Name: DataArcTech/LLM-as-a-Judge
Author List: DataArcTech; companion to A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge by Jiawei Gu, Xuhui Jiang, Zhichao Shi, Hexiang Tan, Xuehao Zhai, Chengjin Xu, Wei Li, Yinghan Shen, Shengjie Ma, Honghao Liu, Yuanzhuo Wang, and Jian Guo
About: Companion repository for the survey A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge. It curates papers, evaluation pipelines, improvement strategies, and meta-evaluation resources for LLM-as-a-judge research.
